html:
Marcopolo is not a “2x earnings” hallucination, but it is still cheap. At the live POMO3 quote of R$5.94, the company is worth about R$7.5 billion, or roughly US$1.5 billion, using the full capital base rather than only the common-share float. That is about 6x trailing earnings and closer to 5x my 2026-2027 earnings power estimate, for a business earning ROE above 30%, paying real cash dividends, and entering 2H26 with far better order visibility than the market feared.

The variant setup is the reversal of the February scare. Investors sold Marcopolo when the school-bus tender was canceled, since Caminho da Escola was roughly 10% of expected 2026 volume. But the program came back, and Marcopolo qualified to supply up to 7,210 units, with impact expected in 2H26 and 2027. That flips the story from “public-program hole” to “backlog visibility into a soft-rate cycle.” The market is still trading the stock like a domestic cyclical whose best year was 2025.

Q1 looked optically mixed, which is why the opportunity still exists. Production fell 9%, revenue slipped 1.3%, and Mexico was weak. Yet EBITDA rose 16.3% to R$304.8 million and net income rose 8.8% to R$264.6 million. Adjusting out the R$45 million NFI one-off, this is not explosive growth yet, but it proves the floor is high: mix, cost control, Australia, parts, Moneo, and equity income are absorbing volume weakness. Marcopolo’s own presentation says Brazil’s bus fleet average age has risen from 8 years in 2012 to 11 years and 4 months in 2025, which supports a multi-year replacement cycle rather than a one-quarter tender trade.

The balance sheet is misunderstood. Screeners show consolidated net debt because Banco Moneo is consolidated, but the industrial business had only R$251 million of net debt, about 0.2x EBITDA, while Moneo’s funding is largely matched against customer receivables. This is not a net-cash liquidation story, and the prior thesis was wrong there. It is a high-return industrial plus captive finance story where leverage optics obscure a very modest industrial risk profile.

The second mislabel is geography. Marcopolo is no longer just a Brazilian bus body assembler waiting for local municipalities. In 2025, international operations plus exports were 45.4% of revenue, up from 36.3% in 2024. Management is explicitly leaning into Latin America, citing Argentina, Peru, Bolivia, and Paraguay, while Volgren in Australia is running ahead of plan. The weaker part of the thesis is that a stronger real hurts exports, and management flagged dollar depreciation as a headwind, but the business is now diversified enough that Brazil school buses, Australian profitability, Latin American exports, Moneo, and NFI recovery can all matter at once.

The retail/social context is useful: Brazilian retail chatter mostly frames POMO as a cheap dividend name or asks why a “cheap” stock keeps falling, with some preference for POMO3 because it is the voting common. That is not a crowded global small-cap compounder narrative. The stock is known locally, but the debate is still about dividend dates, SELIC, and government tenders, not about 2027 normalized earnings power.

My base case is R$1.20 to R$1.30 of 2027 EPS on R$9.5 to R$10.0 billion of revenue, 16.5% to 17.5% EBITDA margin, a normalized NFI contribution, and fuller Caminho da Escola deliveries. Put 7.5x earnings on R$1.25 and the stock is worth about R$9.50. That is not heroic for a company with this ROE, governance level, market position, and dividend capacity.

The biggest risks are a fresh delay in public-program conversion, a Brazil rate cycle that stays tighter for longer, real appreciation that compresses exports, a Mexico/Argentina slump that spreads, or credit deterioration at Moneo. POMO3 liquidity is also lower than POMO4, so the common can carry a practical trading discount despite better voting rights.

Bear target: R$4.50, or about US$0.89, down 24%. This assumes earnings fall toward R$0.75 per share, Caminho deliveries slip, and the market refuses to pay more than 6x depressed earnings.

Base target: R$9.50, or about US$1.87, up 60%. This assumes R$1.20 to R$1.30 forward EPS and a modest rerating to 7.5x as the 2H26 and 2027 backlog becomes visible.

Bull target: R$12.50, or about US$2.47, up 110%. This assumes R$1.45 to R$1.55 EPS from stronger Brazil replacement demand, full school-bus execution, Australia and NFI upside, and an 8x to 8.5x multiple. Current price: R$5.94, about US$1.17.
